data.title: GOVI: Back To The Future
data.description:
Thesis

The Invesco Equal Weight 0-30 Year Treasury ETF (NASDAQ:GOVI) is a fixed income exchange traded fund. We have not covered this name for almost two years, last time when we did the fund was called the Invesco 1-30 Laddered Treasury ETF (PLW) and you can read that research here. The name and ticker changed in August 2023, with the fund now set to track the "ICE 1-30 Year Laddered Maturity US Treasury Index".

The fund represents an elegant way to buy the entire yield curve, and comes with a 10-year duration. Long rates have been under tremendous pressure in the past year, and despite government promises for budget balancing, we seem to be a while away from that.

In today's article we are going to revisit GOVI and try to ascertain if the name is worth buying in today's environment. The article will also explore the risk and rewards associated with the fund and our view on the macro rates picture.

Equal weight composition

The fund contains only treasuries, and pursues an equal weight composition:

Please note the even distribution by maturity date for the fund across the tenor buckets. The composition is in line with the index goals, with the index including up to 30 treasury notes, typically representing the annual February maturity ladder across the yield curve.

The fund composition results in a portfolio with a 10-year duration profile:

The equal weight approach yields an ETF with a 10.3 years effective duration, thus being mainly driven by movements in the 10-year yields. Additional analytics for the fund are:

  • AUM: $0.87 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.15%
  • St Dev: 10.7% (3Y)
  • Sharpe: -0.54

Higher duration than IEF

Many retail investors do not realize that a 10-year treasury note has a lower duration than its stated maturity. It is only normal in a high rates environment since duration is the discounting of all cash-flows, both interest and principal. To that end, the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF) has a lower duration profile than 10 years, namely 7.8 years.

While GOVI and IEF are very well correlated, GOVI is more sensitive to changes in interest rates via its higher duration profile:

As interest rates rose, GOVI lost most in NAV when compared to IEF. Expect the opposite to occur if rates were to shift lower.

Where are 10-year yields going? Back to the future

If we look at the historic picture for long rates we get an interesting graph:

Please note that from 2001 to 2008 (the Great Financial Crisis), 10-year rates navigated a very narrow 4% to 5% range. After the GFC, 4% became resistance for long rates, and then we saw for a decade the lowest long rates on record.

We believe the macro budgetary picture for the United States is bad, and will not change any time soon. Despite all the news reel regarding DOGE, the tangible results are not to be seen:

The red line in the above chart represents the 2025 fiscal deficit, and as one can see it is running above 2024, above 2023 and above 2022. Just coming out on TV and stating that DOGE is making cuts does not actually help tangible numbers. Until the deficit is brought under control, long rates will be under pressure.

We are of the opinion that the U.S. government deficit is like a massive ocean freighter, and to turn it around will take years of work, rather than hours. This leads to a range bound rates picture, with our forecast to move back to what we have seen in the 2001-2008 time-frame, namely a 4% to 5% range.

This view translates into investors starting to buy GOVI when rates are above 4.5% and then selling the name when they move towards the lower bound, namely 4%. Since the fund has a high carry (i.e. yield) of 4.45%, it is an instrument that can be safely held on the balance sheet. We think range trading the name is the ideal choice here:

The above chart is the 10-year yield in the past year. If one simply sold GOVI where the graph presents circles (the bottom of the range in yields - i.e. 4% generally) and bought where the graph has squares (interim tops in yields), one would have done very well.

As investors keep guessing if DOGE works, if spending is under control and inflation is coming back, they will bid rates around in the before mentioned range. We are of the opinion that unless we get a very severe recession, the 4% to 5% range will hold.

What to do at 4.5% yields on 10-year treasuries

As outlined above we believe this is the inflection level for starting to layer into GOVI. Since the level is not the top of the range we are thinking that a DCA approach is best here. An investor can then hold to a high dividend position until we see long rates move back towards the bottom of the range at 4%.

This view on trading the name via the rates range will only change if we get a proper severe recession that will see the Fed cut aggressively and thus shift the entire yield curve much lower, potentially shifting the above range to 3% to 4%. Until then we believe fiscal imbalances and a sticky inflation picture will prove to be crucial in maintaining the range bound price.

Conclusion

GOVI is a fixed income ETF. The name represents a laddered approach on treasuries, with an equal weight composition that results in a fund with a 10-year duration. GOVI only contains treasuries, thus its risk driver is 10-year rates. We believe we are back to an environment akin to 2001-2008 when 10-year rates fluctuated in a tight 4% to 5% range. Unless we get a severe recession, we believe this range will hold. GOVI is to be bought when long rates are above 4.5%, and sold when they touch the bottom of the range around 4%. Investors will be clipping a high 4.45% yield while holding the name.

data.title: Collect 14%, Enjoy Life: Annaly Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly.

If you had the option to put anything behind a "break in case of emergency" glass, what would you put there?

Perhaps you would put a fire axe to be able to chop down a door in the event of a fire, to save lives. Maybe you would put a fire extinguisher to be able to put out a fire in case of an emergency. For some, they may put a defibrillator to be able to restart someone's heart in the event of a cardiac arrest. Others may place a weapon to defend their loved ones in the event of the worst.

Investors will routinely have steps or barriers in their portfolio that will help protect them from the "worst." Unfortunately, for many investors, the worst, in their opinion, is simply a price drop. The price of their holdings drops in value, and they see that as the worst scenario – their money is evaporating before their eyes! So, they put in place steps to try to mitigate that loss. For some, it's a trailing stop loss – if my portfolio dips by 3%, it sells everything, preserving me from a deeper fall or dip. The downside to this is that often these safeguards meant to protect you also become stumbling blocks that stop you from seeing the recovery on the other side.

As an income investor, my goal is to see long-term capital gains, but also to have extremely powerful income in the present. This means that my portfolio needs to provide me with strong income regardless of what is raging around me, whether I'm in an economic boom or an economic bust, I need to have that income to live.

Today, I want to look at an income opportunity that we've covered previously that continues to be heavily misunderstood because the times it shines are when the rest of your portfolio is stumbling. Along with its peers, it can become a "break glass in case of emergency" type holding where it shines and benefits your portfolio the most when the rest of your portfolio is struggling to tread water.

Let's dive in!

Clear Skies Ahead

Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NYSE:NLY), yielding 14.2% according to Seeking Alpha, is the oldest publicly-traded mREIT that focuses on agency MBS. The stock market is very much a "what have you done for me lately?" type of place. It doesn't matter how great an investment was before, the market hates every stock that has underperformed over the past 10 years. Often, when I talk about any stock, I will get comments about "the return over x years is horrible! Why would you ever buy this stock?!?"

Certainly, NLY has fallen into that category. We added NLY to the model portfolio in June of 2019. Its performance was great for the first 8 months, but since that time, it has been quite rough. Relative to the S&P 500, NLY has been a significant underperformer. Relative to (MBB), an ETF that invests in agency MBS, and (TLT), an ETF that invests in 20+ year Treasuries, NLY's performance is delivering the leveraged return it sets out to produce:

You have a leveraged portfolio of agency MBS, and it is going to be impacted by the price movements of agency MBS. NLY's business model performs best during a recession. When the Fed is slashing interest rates, the economy is collapsing, and investors are dumping the S&P 500 (SP500).

Those conditions haven't occurred. Instead, we've seen the S&P 500 trade at very high valuations, interest rates get hiked at the fastest pace since the 1980s, and while the economy has been slowing, the market isn't quite willing to call it a recession yet.

NLY saw headwinds both to its book value and to its cash flow as the yield curve inverted, reducing the profitability of borrowing short-term and lending long-term.

We can see how much NLY's portfolio has changed over the past 3 years. Here are NLY's MBS holdings by coupon in Q1 2022: Source.

Here is NLY's Q1 2025 MBS holdings by coupon: Source.

As debt yields go up, that means the prices of previously outstanding debt go down because investors will need to get larger capital gains to settle for the lower coupon.

There is still some volatility in the MBS market, but compared to what it went through from 2022 to 2023, there is a clear stabilization. Source.

We will see some price swings, but the big collapse is in the rearview mirror.

NLY has taken advantage of this relative stability to increase the size of its portfolio. NLY's investment portfolio is back over $100 billion for the first time since 2020:

NLY owns more assets, and those assets are paying higher coupons. That's why NLY raised its dividend to $0.70 in Q1, and in the earnings call confirmed that they expect to keep it at that level the rest of the year. If we see more Fed rate cuts and/or rising MBS prices, we believe it is possible that we see another dividend increase in 2025.

NLY is more diversified than some peers, with 21% of its capital invested in MSRs (Mortgage Servicing Rights) and non-agency residential credit:

In terms of cash flow, the agency MBS capital has a lot more leverage, and is therefore, overwhelmingly responsible for cash flow. The primary impact from an earnings standpoint is on book value.

NLY's book value for Q1 was at $19.02, down only 0.7% from Q4.

This compares to peer AGNC which saw book value decline 2.0% during the same period. The primary difference is that NLY's MSR portfolio offset some of the price declines of the MBS portfolio.

Overall, we expect agency MBS to be the primary driver of NLY's fortunes, and looking forward that is a strong positive. Agency MBS prices are stabilizing, and the environment is safer for agency mREITs to be buying and leveraging up. Right now, stable interest rates are enough to drive growing cash flow. To the extent that we see a recession, we could see an earnings boom similar to what NLY experienced during the GFC and during the Dot-com bust.

The agency MBS trade isn't always going to outperform the S&P 500. What makes NLY such a valuable part of a portfolio is the timing of when it outperforms: Such as 2000-2004:

Or 2007-2011:

NLY outperforms the most during recessions. That's why NLY is in my portfolio, and it doesn't hurt that I'm collecting a hefty dividend while I wait.

Conclusion

Every holding in your portfolio should have a specific purpose or reason for being there. There shouldn't be a holding in your portfolio that is there simply for diversification's sake. It should have a principal reason for being there on its own.

NLY has a purpose within an income portfolio. Its purpose is to be the superhero that arrives when catastrophe strikes, providing you with outsized income and returns when the rest of your portfolio is struggling to do so. This is one massive benefit of having a highly diversified portfolio. You can have holdings that are benefiting while other holdings are struggling and do not need to try gaming or timing which ones to hold.

Your retirement is going to be wonderful. At least I hope for you that it will be. Your retirement will span decades that may see economic fortunes or economic misfortunes for the nation around you. You do not want your portfolio to be tossed and turned by the winds that are blowing in the economy. You want your portfolio to be a bedrock of income provision for you and your loved ones. This way, you can enjoy the opportunities that are bound around you and not have to hide in fear of the storm.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Micron Q3 Preview: Good Setup For An Option Play
data.description:
MU stock: Q3 earnings expected on June 25

I last analyzed Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) on March 26, 2025. That article was titled "Micron: How I Trade Cyclical Stocks". The analysis focused on the status of its business cycle and concluded with a HOLD rating based on the following factors:

Investors might be tempted to do some bottom fishing with Micron Technology, Inc.'s stock's recent price dip. The current low P/E adds further alure. However, I want to remind investors that P/E is a horrible leading indicator for cyclical stocks like MU. Furthermore, since my last writing, some new catalysts have emerged and could pressure MU's profit margin going forward.

Since that writing, there have been a few new catalysts that are worth updating. These developments include both macroscopic geopolitics and also company-specific issues. This article will focus on the top changes in my view, which include A) the ongoing tariff negotiations and the latest development on the AI diffusion rule for the former bucket, and B) the upcoming Q3 earnings report [ER] for the company. My thesis is that the options market is underestimating the uncertainties brought about by these catalysts judging by the average implied volatility ("IV"). As such, I believe the current conditions represent a good setup for an option play. Potential investors can buy call option to gain exposure with minimal upfront capital commitment. Existing investors can buy put options as a cost-effective hedge against negative surprises.

To set the stage for the rest of the discussion, let me start with a recap of MU's last quarter's earnings report [ER] and a brief preview of its next ER. As you can see from the following chart, MU demonstrated strong performance in its last ER announced on March 20, 2025. It achieved an EPS Normalized of $1.56, which beat estimates by $0.14. The EPS GAAP and total revenue also exceeded the consensus estimates. Looking ahead, the upcoming quarter's ER is scheduled on June 25, 2025. The EPS Normalized is projected to be $1.59 and an EPS GAAP is estimated to be $1.43, translating into a sequential growth of 1.9% and 1.4%, respectively. Total revenue for the upcoming quarter is estimated to be $8.83 billion, translating into a sequential growth of 9.6%, substantially faster than the EPS growth.

Therefore, here I see the first uncertainty - the balance between growth and profitability. The good news here is that growth is expected to continue, but the bad news is that the net profit margin is likely to be under pressure as the top line grows at a much faster pace than the bottom line.

Such, uncertainties extend to the other areas as well, as detailed next.

MU stock: gross margin uncertainty

In general, I feel positive about MU's opportunity in the emerging AI market and high-bandwidth memory market. But thus far, I have not seen definitive indicators yet that this opportunity is leading to improved profitability. In addition to the net profit margin, MU's gross margin remains under pressure too, despite record sales from AI tailwinds and the anticipated growth ahead as mentioned above.

As a broader background, the chart provided below illustrates MU quarterly gross profit margin over the past several years. As of the latest data point from its FY 2025 Q1, the gross profit margin stands at 36.79%, it is notably above the average gross profit margin in the past 5 years of 25.40% (the good news). But it is also far below its peak margin of 47.28% sustained during 2021~2022 and also slightly lower than that in the previous quarter as seen.

Looking ahead, I see some conflicting forces that could keep the direction of its margins (both gross and net) uncertainty. Top factors on the negative side include unfavorable pricing, a mixed shift to the consumer end-market (e.g., mobile), and continued headwinds from NAND underutilization. On the positive side, once the company moves past the startup costs associated with its Idaho fab and newer DRAM node, I expect the margins to start rebounding. Furthermore, as to be detailed later, the revised AI diffusion rule from the Trump administration could also help.

MU stock: implied volatilities and options

Given the above considerations, I believe the prevailing IV underling MU options is relatively low and underestimates the true uncertainties entailed by the stock. As such, the current conditions represent a favorable setup for option buyers. As an example, as seen from the following statistic taken from MarketChameleon, the current IV of MU is only on part with its historical average. To wit, the current reading is 46.7, placing it in the 44th percentile rank only (i.e., almost on average). Furthermore, the current IV of 46.7 is 17.5% lower than the 20-day historical volatility [HV] of 56.6. In other words, the current IV is noticeably lower than the stock's realized volatility.

Due to the mild IV, MU's options are attractively priced for buyers in my model. For example, the following option chain (taken from Seeking Alpha) displays a few specific call options for MU. Note that I specifically picked an expiration date of Jun 27, 2025, the day after its anticipated Q3 ER release, so that these call options can accommodate any potential earnings surprises. As a specific example, an NTM call option (near-the-money) with a strike price of $96 last traded with a price of $5.9. For readers new to options, each option is based on 100 shares, and the table below quotes pricing on a per share basis. The option premium is thus only about 6% of MU's current stock prices, allowing investors want MU exposure to gain exposure with little upfront capital layout (say, compared to buying shares directly). Similarly, current MU shareholders can consider put options as a cost-effective insurance for their existing MU position.

Other risks and final thoughts

There are a few other uncertainties that are worth mentioning and further widen the gap between the IV perceived by the option market and the real uncertainties facing MU.

As mentioned earlier, the revised AI diffusion rule from the Trump administration could also help with MU's margins going forward. For readers unfamiliar with this development, the following news lease from the Department of Commerce should provide sufficient background. I don't expect the rescission of the Biden-era framework and or the anticipated new framework to have a direct and/or immediate impact on specific products from MU. But I do expect potential indirect benefits. If the new framework leads to a broader global expansion of AI development and opens up new markets for AI hardware made in the U.S., this could increase the overall demand for the memory and storage solutions that MU provides.

Department of Commerce, WASHINGTON, D.C. (May 13, 2025) - Today, the Department of Commerce [DOC] announced a rescission of the Biden Administration's AI Diffusion Rule, while rolling out additional steps to strengthen export controls on semiconductors worldwide.

The AI Diffusion Rule was issued on January 15, 2025, with compliance requirements that were set to come into effect on May 15, 2025. These new requirements would have stifled American innovation and saddled companies with burdensome new regulatory requirements. The AI Diffusion Rule also would have undermined U.S. diplomatic relations with dozens of countries by downgrading them to second-tier status.

As a final reflection of the uncertainties MU is facing, the chart below shows consensus estimates for its EPS growth (and also the implied FWD P/E) in the next few years. You can see that the projections exhibit large variance and gyrations. For full fiscal 2025, the estimated EPS varies from a low of $6.03 to a high of $7.89. The mid-point estimate is $6.99 and implies a substantial year-over-year growth of 437.56%. The projections for FY 2025 are even more spread out, with EPS estimated varying from a low of $6.28 to a high of $15.75. Also note that based on the midpoint of 2025 EPS estimate, the forward P/E is about 13.7, representing a moderate level of valuation risks compared to the stock's median P/E of 11x to 12x.

To recap, this follow-up on MU is motivated by both macroscopic factors (in particular the rescission of the Biden administration's AI diffusion rule) and also MU's upcoming Q3 ER. Overall, I see both positives and negatives in these developments, resulting in a hold rating. Furthermore, I see an attractive set-up for the use of option given these developments. The use of options is part of the strategies our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) uses for both hedging and leveraging purposes. Under MU's current conditions, I think the current options market is underestimating the fundamental uncertainties entailed by MU.

data.title: A Real Estate IPO Could Substantially Eclipse Leon's Furniture EV
data.description:
Liquidity comfortably above 300k USD in daily volume on the Toronto listing for the last three months and beyond.

Leon's Furniture (TSX:LNF:CA)(OTCPK:LEFUF) is an interesting prospect. The company is pursuing a project to monetise its real estate assets that are worth something approaching the company's current market cap if not significantly more. In this respect it resembles a past idea, Correios de Portugal (OTCPK:CTTPY), which was one of our successful investments of 2024 that similarly put a value on its extensive real estate portfolio to catalyse its stock.

What will be left on a proforma basis is a slightly less profitable (due to the presence of rent expenses) furniture retailing business based in Canada. Nonetheless, the company's equity is cheap enough that even on a price ignoring the value of the real estate, the FCFY was above 14% in 2024 and 2023. Even if they had rent expenses proforma, we think that the FCFY would remain above 10% on current prices, assuming the operation doesn't decline on macro headwinds.

We think the real estate monetisation plans are an attractive follow up to several years now of buybacks, accumulating to around 14% over the past years, again a consciousness of the fact that the company's equity has been trading below its intrinsic value for quite a while.

As a note, the real estate is planned to be monetised through an IPO. That would be best for shareholders if possible, but it seems a spinoff is also on the table though that would leave it to the market to value those shares. An IPO would be more definitive if the primary market allows it. Rates didn't get cut as hoped by some just now on ongoing inflation fear from tariffs.

While there are some risks, mainly to the Canadian economy in the form of retail sales pressures and of course GDP pressure all related to the demand hit that comes from worsened terms of trade with the major trading partner of the US on account of tariffs (as well as purchasing power hit from Canada's own tariffs on the US), there is a value play here and retail sales by the company are holding up as of the very recent Q1 data. We expect announcements to start around the key real estate development, which is partially a residential use development planned in Toronto, as well as monetising their other industrial assets to begin in a couple of months, and to culminate into a spinoff into a REIT eventually, as they have already announced, probably when the rate situation becomes more favourable for primary market demand. That does mean that LFL does depend quite a bit on a rate cutting angle in Canada, where rate cuts have not continued as of the latest meeting despite questions around GDP impacts from tariffs, but will likely come eventually.

Real Estate Angle

Before we discuss the operating business, let's focus on the real estate angle. A major development will be of the 40 acres of land near the 401 and 400 highways in Toronto. This is not on the fringes of Toronto, it is quite central. They plan on building a new flagship store here as well as the corporate HQ. Their home office has been here since the company went public but they want to revamp the parcel in order to also sell 4000 residential units that they will develop on the property. LFL have experience with building properties, as they have built showrooms and even the industrial real estate for themselves since the beginning, which is why they have such a massive real estate portfolio now. They have already received approval from the province for regeneration zoning of the parcel. The secondary plan for this parcel is expected to be complete in mid-2025 together with the City of Toronto. The plan for this development has been in the works for a few years but was put on the backburner during COVID-19 and also in the wake of the Brick acquisition a decade ago where the company had been focused on deleveraging and optimising the new footprint. Currently, 6 acres of the 40 acres is in use for retail and warehouse space which will get bulldozed to start from scratch to more fully utilise the 40 acres for the planned residential and retail/commercial purposes.

This is just one development where they have plans to make residential units. In aggregate they have 430 acres of land with mixed-used space mostly industrial and retail, as well as a lot of excess and undeveloped land. 41% of the acreage is in Ontario, 27% Alberta and 9% for Quebec and 9% for BC. The GTA is 22% of the acreage. The company acknowledges that the market for industrial assets remains pretty good, and that they perceive a NAV discount with their stock and are looking to also monetise all the real estate assets through a REIT. They also believe that they can move retail space from industrial nodes on property that they own to leased locations instead and open up the owned space for new developments where possible. These plans are all immediate considerations for the company and it's their top priority to pursue this monetisation and help markets appreciate the value that's embedded in the stock.

This is our reckoning for the valuation.

  • The specific 40 acres in Toronto could be worth upwards of CAD$0.66-0.9 billion in net value over investment just based on the residential component (4000 residential units around CAD$500-700k each at selling price, assuming around a standard real estate 1.5x MOIC, so net value is 33% of the total selling price of the units)
  • Acreage outside of GTA including industrial assets could be worth as much as CAD$6 billion (going off industrial multiples per square meter converted to acres from other developed markets like Japan, net of costs and with a MOIC of around 1.5x which should be normal for real estate, applied to the LFL acreage that isn't the specific 40 acre Toronto parcel). Crudely, you could also look at it as 10x the value of the 40 acres in Toronto, applied to the remaining ~400 acres of the acres owned by LFL. While property outside of Toronto might be less valuable, the assets on these other properties are already in place and don't require redevelopment like the Toronto parcel.
  • The rest of the GTA acreage could be worth another CAD$0.66-0.9 billion based on being the other half of the LFL exposure in Toronto

That gets us to a crude estimate of up to CAD$7.8 billion in real estate assets. Obviously, that's a lot compared to CAD$1.7 billion market cap. We felt a little uncomfortable with the discrepancy and did sanity check these figures in conversation with some friends in Canadian REPE, and they said maybe CAD$0.4 billion for the 40 acres and around CAD$1 billion for the rest based on today's bidding environment. So CAD$1.4 billion in total. That's up against around the same in EV (under $1.7 billion) for Leon's when ignoring RE in the equity bridge (ends up a pretty negligible EV). We'll go ahead with these attenuated figures for the sake of conservatism, but even our values should be possible in the bull case, but these wide differentials are inevitable when discussing private and quite heterogenous and illiquid assets.

Discussing the Operating Business

The operating business is retail of appliances, mattresses and furniture, as well as related warranties and insurance. This business is rather fragmented in Canada, with still half of the market being medium-sized and mom and pop shops.

More and more of the warranty-related repairs now happen in house in order to improve margin on those underwritten policies. You can look at the broken out insurance and extended warranty revenue which was closer to CAD$100 million for the FY, running similarly in the Q1 on a quarterly basis. Similarly, franchise sales are pretty limited at around CAD$33 million against CAD$2.5 billion in revenues. So it remains a pretty straightforward retailing business, mostly not franchised.

The results are the following for the FY and for the Q1. The revenue is higher in the Q4 compared to the average run-rate this year, meaning that retail trends aren't too bad though it has been the Christmas season in these last results which should be a seasonal kick. Apparently there were some signs of softness in consumer spending and a mail strike prevented mail-based marketing of Black Friday sales that might have dinged the YoY comps in an important season. Sequentially revenues were up from the previous quarter where it was CAD$651 million, but this should be no surprise given the modest seasonality in the business.

Retail sales are under a little bit of pressure and were heading in the wrong direction as of the FY. The reason is of course the general economic concerns around the Canadian economy, both from tariffs on US goods and the demand hit caused by the US tariffs on a good amount of Canadian goods. Focusing just on Ontario, around 20% of imports and exports from the US will be affected by some kind of tariff, which will have a considerable impact on purchasing power and has shaved off some bps from GDP forecasts for the Canadian economy. Shipping delays of some furniture inventory also didn't help the FY figures particularly into holidays. The bulk of tariffs didn't start until after Christmas 2024. Markets therefore were delighted by the positive trends shown by the Q1 results with growth YoY. While there was a sequential hit from Q4 due to seasonal effects, gross profit and revenue clearly grew YoY.

EBITDA was relatively flat when accounting for the gain on settlement for the FY. Note also that the quantity of stores stayed almost exactly the same in the FY. In the latest quarter it was up thanks to the gross margin and sales improvements.

Longer-term, Canadian demographics are also decent, but fertility rates and family formation rates are low like most developed countries. Nonetheless, the forecast and inevitable population growth should fuel demand for furniture longer-term, assuming minimalism doesn't offset population growth for demand to grow.

Valuation

Inventories and trade receivables declined, with the inventory decline being partially unwanted due to shipping delays. The cash consequences were really positive though with the FCFY in 2024 being around 17%. On a proforma basis, we think that the leases may eat into operating cash flows by around CAD$75 million. If you take a midpoint between 2024 and 2023 cash generation then the figure is above 10% proforma. Taking 2024 run-rates as is and assuming leases once the properties are disposed gives an FCFY of around 14%, which is still very high. The Q1 data is useless for cash flows due to the seasonality of working capital investments.

We think around 10% proforma is a reasonable guess as to where the business will fall after the disposals which is a clear sign that there is objective cash value in this business, even ignoring the real estate angle.

For the valuation, we used EBIT proforma post-disposal but based on TTM data. We got the assumed proforma rent expense with a 5% cap rate on the total value of the real estate. We used the more conservative figures for the real estate valuations. In the past, albeit during COVID-19 which was a remodelling cycle, got EBIT up to around CAD$300 million, or proforma around CAD$225 million, so much higher than our assumptions. We comped based on EV/EBIT mutliples. We also accounted for possible tax effects on the sale, where we assumed for simplicity's sake that book values were essentially zero, though this figure is very tentative and we are not tax experts.

Risks

The valuation will obviously be appealing once you factor in the real estate value. However, there are clearly risks. Furniture businesses suffer in a downturn, and this is an industry that has featured many bankruptcies. There is another issue which is also how the IPO of the REIT containing the Leon properties will materialise. With the rate cutting paused in Canada pending the possible inflationary effects of tariffs, the immediate interest can't be that high for real estate whose success, particularly on the development side, is sensitive to the rate situation. Remember, the primary market for these assets, ostensibly investors in Canada, have to be interested in a REIT offering for the monetisation of these assets to happen in a way that would actually benefit Leon's shareholders at the valuations we describe above. As much help as possible from lower rates would be good to increase the likelihood of the catalyst coming to pass. Nonetheless, the company will likely start moving towards the development ambitions as soon as possible as it concerns the 40 acres in central Toronto, particularly as the planning is already underway with the city, despite the immediate rate environment.

The other thing to mention is that the business is family controlled. There are quite a few family controlled businesses on Canadian markets that are quite undervalued. However, given the current rate of payout being reasonably high at over 60%, and given the buyback history, we don't think that appropriate capital allocation and shareholder payouts following a punctuated investing cash inflow are unlikely. Certainly more buybacks could be possible, and Miller-Modigliani would say that's just as good as a straight dividend.

Conclusions

Ultimately, the company is cheap and management knows it. Buybacks have been significant these past years and still continue with a 5% buyback programme instituted in March, and the dividend isn't bad at current levels and reflects a general policy of shareholder payouts that benefits the minority shareholders as well as the primary shareholders alike. With the company unlikely to refunnel proceeds from a real estate IPO into the business, at least not to a great extent as it is after all a furniture business with limited growth and they already did an important consolidation with Brick some years ago, a significant dividend could be possible using the proceeds from an IPO of a REIT containing their real estate, if not a major buyback which is also likely to be received positively by markets. The leadership at LFL used to work at Canadian Tire (OTCPK:CDNTF) which did the same REIT spin off move before raising payouts in late 2013.

The fact that the underlying operating business isn't dragging even though the Q1 reflects a tariff-impacted environment, since the tariff spat between Canada and the US started earlier than the spats between US and the rest of the world, is also a positive sign that at least for enough time for the real estate plans to go ahead, the operating business won't be a reason for the company to remain eschewed by value investors. We also think that modest growth remains secularly possible not just on demographics but on the clear operational advantage that a larger player like Leon's has over mom and pop furniture businesses which are still most prevalent in the fragmented Canadian market. We like the prospects here.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition - open to all analysts - is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MongoDB's Growth Engine Stalls: Atlas Deceleration Signals Deeper Problems Ahead (Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

MongoDB’s (NASDAQ:MDB) stock is down 34% since my Buy rating in November. The NoSQL database leader was seeing robust growth (27%) from MongoDB Atlas (a database-as-a-service) at that time, but faced notable competition from Amazon (AMZN), Oracle (ORCL), and Google (GOOGL). A challenge of MongoDB’s was that despite being in business for nearly two decades, it has yet to achieve consistent free cash flow (FCF), thanks to large S&M and R&D investments. I noted that these investments were necessary to remain relevant in this market. After performing a “reverse” discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis, I ascertained that MongoDB needed to achieve, on average, revenue growth and FCF margins of 20% and 15%, respectively, to justify its valuation.

The following article revisits the stock to see if its slump is merely market noise or a signal that MongoDB’s business is in trouble.

Historical Trends

When I evaluate high-growth, technology companies, I am interested in how efficiently and sustainably they are growing. Looking back at MongoDB’s financial performance over the past five (fiscal) years, it scores a “Moderate” on my Growth Quality Dashboard, which evaluates seven critical metrics.

There are some concerning trends. For starters, MDB’s revenue growth is rapidly decelerating. Notably, this figure was just 19.22% for its fiscal year ending January 2025 (as represented by “2024” in the Growth Efficiency line chart above). This fell just short of my reverse DCF estimate of 20% and likely explains why the stock has underperformed since my last evaluation. Granted, a 35.8% CAGR is still solid, and the deceleration could simply be a maturing market. However, should the trend continue, I think it’s clear evidence that MDB is losing ground to its competitors. Moreover, although capital intensity has improved, MDB is still bogged down by heavy asset requirements relative to its peers in SaaS. R&D intensity is declining, but the absolute value has increased, and ~30% is a significant investment.

Valuation Assumptions

A few assumptions have changed since my last DCF analysis on MDB. For starters, its beta is much higher, coming in at 1.49 (5Y Monthly). Second, my terminal growth percentage (“perpetual growth rate”) estimate of 4 may have been too kind. 3% is more standard. “Year 1” revenue of $2.27 billion represents the consensus analyst estimate for MDB’s fiscal 2026. Revenue growth and FCF margin of 20% and 15%, respectively, represent averages during the nine-year projection. MDB’s recent FCF margin has been in the mid-to-high single digits, so 15% is a bit optimistic, but reasonably achievable for a SaaS company. Everything else is pretty standard.

So, despite its stock being down 48% in the past year, MDB is still quite expensive on a cash flow basis. Other metrics support this notion as well.

Given the deceleration of growth, MDB’s premium valuation is puzzling.

During my last look, MongoDB Atlas was seeing robust growth. However, in its fourth quarter fiscal 2025 report, Atlas's growth dropped to 24% year-over-year, making up 71% of total revenue. Total revenue was $548.4 million (up 20% year-over-year). Gross profit margin was 73%, compared to 75% a year prior. The company did generate $50.5 million in cash from operations, but this was no improvement over the same period in the prior year.

For its full year fiscal 2026, MongoDB is guiding for $2.24 billion and $2.28 billion. This would represent ~13% year-over-year growth. Notably, its non-Atlas business “will be down in the high-single digits for the year.” As for its first quarter guidance, MongoDB expects “Atlas revenue to be flat to slightly up sequentially.” This was impacted by “typical seasonality” and “holidays.”

Back to my DCF model, the following sensitivity analysis reveals the growth and margin expectations implied by MDB's valuation.

The path to outperformance is narrow. The market is pricing MDB for 27.2% growth. Even if margins improve to 22%, growth expectations remain over 20%.

Finally, I ran a Monte Carlo simulation to account for slight changes to key variables such as growth, margin, and beta. This gives us a more realistic and dynamic view of share price outcomes.

With the current assumptions, MDB is not merely overvalued, but grossly overvalued. This is evidenced by its current price of $191.29, trading over the 95th percentile of $183.96.

As for growth levers, MongoDB acquired Voyage AI “to enable organizations to build trustworthy AI applications” in February for $200 million. This will essentially serve to improve MongoDB’s existing platform and is not expected to contribute much to revenue in its fiscal year 2026.

Conclusion

To wrap things up, MongoDB’s growth is decelerating much faster than I anticipated, and this extends to Atlas, which is projected for ~20% growth. During its earnings call, MongoDB reiterated that its fiscal year 2026 will be a “transition year.” I may be a pessimist, but I think this is a subtle signal of things to come. To put it simply, things are not trending in the right direction. In parallel, market expectations remain sky-high (as evidenced by MDB's premium valuation) and out of touch. It’s hard to justify buying MDB in light of its issues (rating downgrade to “Hold” and “market underperform”). The growth deceleration points to market share erosion, and MDB's valuation is too high. Still, I can see MDB utility in a barbell portfolio, but this more like a speculative AI play than a strong and sustainable business.

data.title: Pan American Silver: Strengthened By MAG Silver Acquisition
data.description:
Introduction

With the current uncertainties in the market, there has been a switch to assets viewed as safe havens. Among them is gold (GLD), traditionally seen as the ultimate haven assets in times of uncertainty. Since the beginning of the year, the price of gold has risen 21.5%, resulting in a rise of 35% for the past year.

With a rising gold price comes a boost for gold miners. One such miner that has benefited is Pan American Silver (NYSE:PAAS). Whilst it has silver in its name, Pan American derives the majority of its revenue from the production of gold. Since my previous coverage last September, the shares have risen 6.7%, significantly ahead of the S&P 500, but lagging the growth seen in precious metal prices.

Whilst Pan American Silver’s jurisdictional profile does not appear the best at first glance, with only one mine in a tier-one jurisdiction [Canada], its other mines are spread across 6 jurisdictions. This results in it being one of the most geographically diversified precious metal miners.

With a solid set of results released for the first quarter of 2025, precious metal prices remaining high, and a recent acquisition of MAG Silver (MAG), let’s explore whether Pan American Silver remains a buy at this price.

Q1 Earnings

Pan American Silver reported its Q1-25 earnings at the start of May, reporting quarterly production of 5.0 million ounces of silver and 182,200 ounces of gold. This translated to no change in silver production compared to last year, but an 18% fall in gold production.

Gold production saw a dip, largely due to its disposition of the La Arena Mine in Peru in December 2024, contributing an 18,700-ounce reduction. In addition, lower grades at the Minera Florida Mine (2.29 g/t of gold vs. 2.97 g/t of gold) and Shahuindo Mine (0.44 g/t of gold vs. 0.50 g/t of gold), plus the Dolores mine entering a residual leaching phase following cessation of mining last July led to lower production. The other mines in the gold segment also reported smaller falls in grade and throughput.

In the silver segment, production was flat. Production fell at both the Cerro Moro mine and San Vicente mine due to mine sequencing leading to lower grade areas being mined. However, this was offset by the Huaron mine which, despite lower grades, reported increased production as throughput increased. Also increasing production was the La Colorada mine which saw 0.28 million ounces higher production driven by higher throughput following the completion of new ventilation infrastructure.

Despite production of silver being flat and gold production falling, revenues actually came in 29% higher than last year at $773.2 million. This was down to Pan American benefitting from a record realized gold price of $2,868 per ounce (+36% YoY) and higher realized silver prices of $31.25 per ounce (+38% YoY).

Based on this quarter's production, Pan American reaffirmed it remains on track to meet its 2025 outlook for gold and silver production of 735 to 800 thousand ounces and 20 to 21 million ounces respectively.

Pan American Silver reported a cash flow from operations of $240.1 million in the first quarter, an almost doubling from $127.1 million in the same quarter a year earlier. This translated into a free cash flow of $112.6 million. On the balance sheet side, Pan American had $923 million in cash and cash equivalents at quarter end, and a total debt of $804.4 million. This meant Pan American has a net-cash position indicating a very strong balance sheet.

Continuing to costs and margins, the company reported a first quarter all in sustaining cost [AISC] of $13.94 per ounce for the silver segment (-12% YoY) and $1,485 per ounce for the gold segment (-6% YoY).

The decrease in silver segment AISC was largely driven by the improved ventilation infrastructure at La Colorada leading to lower mining costs per tonne of ore and higher silver production. Cerro Moro mine saw lower AISC due the higher gold price increasing byproduct credits, with Huaron AISC also benefitting from increased byproduct credits from higher zinc and lead production. This fall in silver AISC was partially offset by a $2.08 increase in AISC per ounce at the San Vicente mine due to higher royalties and lower zinc byproduct credits.

In the gold segment, AISC also fell driven by the Dolores mine ceasing mining activities with Q1 2025 costs largely reflecting residual leaching activities which led to a much lower AISC. AISC rose significantly at Minera Flores (+35%) and Shahuindo (+18%) largely due to the lower grades produced. With the higher metal prices in the quarter, and lower AISC, this led to an increase in AISC margins.

Overall, this translated into net earnings of $169.3 million, compared to a net loss of $30.8 million a year earlier. Adjusted earnings also rose coming in at $153.0 million, up from $4.7 million last year and equivalent to earnings per share of $0.47. Pan American had a strong start to the year with the rising precious metal prices boosting earnings. We are seeing the new ventilation infrastructure at La Colorada pay off helping set Pan American up for what I believe will be a strong year.

MAG Silver Purchase

Like any commodity producer there is a finite life to Pan American Silver’s mines, with the eventual depletion of reserves inevitable. The Dolores mine has already shifted to a residual leaching following the end of mining and the Minera Florida mine has only a few years left of production based on current reserves.

Whilst Pan American has the large Escobal mine with over 250 million ounces of reserves and the potential for 20 million ounces a year of production, production has been suspended following a court ruling in Guatemala. The company continues its ILO 169 consultation process and meetings with the Guatemalan government, but progress has been slow, with no date for the restart of production.

With no plan for Escobal production restarting soon, Pan American faces a potential drop in production by the end of this decade. Whilst higher gold and silver prices may allow life extensions at some mines, new mines were required. This led Pan American Silver to recently announce the acquisition of MAG Silver in a $2.1 billion transaction.

For those less familiar, MAG Silver holds a 44% interest in the Juanicipio Mine, a high grade silver mine located in Zacatecus, Mexico. The remaining 56% is owned by the mine operator Fresnillo (OTCPK:FNLPF). Juanicipio is one of the most profitable silver mines globally, with an AISC for 2025 forecast to be between $6 to $8. With proven & probable reserves of over 15 million tonnes at 628 g/t of silver equivalent, this is a high grade, low cost mine, with a initial 13 year mine life before we even consider the huge potential exploration upside.

Production is estimated to be 14.7 to 16.7 million ounces of Silver in 2025, with roughly 7 million ounces attributable to MAG Silver, on par with Pan Americans La Colorada mine, but at significantly better margins. At current spot prices, Juanicipio is expected to generate $98 million in cash flow for the 44% stake held.

Of course, the most important thing is the price paid. Pan American is paying $2.1 billion made up of $500 million in cash and 0.755 Pan American shares per MAG Silver Shares. With 15.4 million tonnes of Proven and Probable Mineral Reserves at grades of 248 g/t silver, 1.58 g/t gold, 2.64% lead and 4.80% zinc [628 g/t silver equivalent (AgEq)], this gives roughly 341.1 million ounces of silver in reserves for the Juanicipio mine. With a 44% stake in the mine, this attributes 150.1 million ounces of reserves to MAG Silver meaning Pan American paid $14 per ounce of silver equivalent reserves. Whilst this is higher than the roughly $10 per ounce Pan American Silver paid in 2018 for Tahoe Resources, it is significantly lower than the $25.70 per ounce reserves that peer Coeur Mining (CDE) paid for SilverCrest last year for a mine in the same jurisdiction [Mexico] and at slightly higher AISC.

In addition to the 44% stake in Juanicipio mine, the purchase of MAG Silver also adds 2 additional 100% owned exploration assets: Deer Trail in Utah and Larder in Ontario providing additional optionality in the future should exploration be successful. It is important to note that even the 7,679 hectare Juanicipio property is only 10% explored, with high-grade intercepts reported in step-out and regional drilling, giving potential long-term exploration upside.

Overall, this appears to be a good purchase for Pan American Silver. Whilst it cost $500 million in cash and 0.755 Pan American shares [roughly 78.5 million new shares issued increasing the share count by 21%] it comes at the same time as Pan American has been performing buybacks, authorizing buybacks of up to 5% of issued shares in 2025.

Given the price paid, the mine economics, and free cash flow Juanicipio will produce, this looks like an accreditive acquisition despite the share dilution. Juanicipio is a high-quality asset with industry leading margins, and a long life potential, something Pan American mines currently lack. With significant further upside possible from further exploration, I believe this is a good acquisition for Pan American Silver and helps position the company for a strong future.

Valuation

With a metal miner’s earnings being heavily reliant on the price of the underlying commodity they mine, an exact value for the miner will vary depending on the metal's price. In times of higher precious metal prices, all miners stand to benefit. However, each miner has a different starting value and operations that will impact future returns. As such, I believe one way to get the relative value of Pan American Silver is in comparison to its precious metal mining peers such as Coeur Mining (CDE), Endeavor Silver (EXK), Silvercorp Metals (SVM) AngloGold Ashanti (AU), Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), Barrick Mining (B), Hecla Mining (HL), Fresnillo (OTCPK:FNLPF) and First Majestic Silver (AG). Whilst all of these have significant precious metal operations, they all have large differences in cash and debt levels, so a direct comparison of earnings multiples will not suffice, and I instead use an enterprise value to EBITDA multiple.

Looking at the above we can see that Pan American Silver trades at an EV to EBITDA multiple of 5.70. This places it roughly middle of the pack compared to peers. However, we must also remember that Pan American is amongst one of the highest quality miners in this group with significant diversification with mines in over 6 jurisdictions, significant scale compared to smaller peers, and a history of strong management. When we consider that this metric is before interest and Pan American has a net cash position whereas peers have net debt positions leading to higher interest costs, Pan American appears even better. Despite trading in the middle of the pack, I am willing to argue that this is perfectly fair, and if anything, a slight premium could be justified.

To determine a price value for Pan American Silver, I calculate the estimated free cash flow and apply a multiple to this. I use the midpoint of guided production for 767,500 ounces of gold and 20.5 million ounces of silver given the company seems on track and the midpoint guided AISC. I assume a tax rate of 30%.

Assuming gold at $3,000 an ounce and silver at $32 an ounce, just below the current precious metal price, we get a free cash flow of $927 million in 2025. I apply a free cash flow multiplier of 10.7, the average of the mining sector [9.88] and Pan American’s 5-year average multiple [11.51], which gives a market cap of $9.9 billion. With 362.2 million shares currently outstanding, this gives a target price of $27.33. With the shares currently trading at $22.74 per share, Pan American appears to be undervalued.

If we assume the takeover of MAG Silver will result in the share count increasing to 440.5 million shares, this will translate to a target price of $22.47 per share, near the current share price. However, this fails to take into account the additional free cash flow produced by the Juanicipio mine which at an estimated $100 million in 2025 alone adds an additional $2.42 to the target share price making it $24.89. This is before we even consider the huge potential future upside the acquisition of MAG Silver presents from further mine expansion, exploration, and higher free cash flow in future years. Whilst it may impact the cash flow multiple derived target in the short term, in the long term it is likely to be highly accreditive.

It is important to remember that these calculations are done at precious metal prices lower than current prices, suggesting these calculations are more likely to be an underestimate. Additionally, I do not take into account the currently ongoing share buybacks which again boost the target share price. With the shares trading in-line with peers when a slight premium could be justified, and my calculations suggesting Pan American is undervalued at this price, I continue to believe that Pan American is a buy.

As with any investment, investing in Pan American Silver is not without its risk. With the company’s revenue and earnings dependence on the price of its underlying metals, I believe this is the primary risk. Any significant reduction in the price of gold, silver, or other byproducts will significantly reduce earnings and drive the share price lower. A further important risk is the mixed jurisdictional profile Pan American operates in. Whilst Canada may present little risk as a tier one, their other jurisdictions expose Pan American to sudden regulatory changes and political uncertainty, which could negatively impact operations and hence profitability. Just look at what happened with Pan American’s Escobal mine in Guatemala. One mitigation of this risk is the highly diversified nature of Pan American’s operations with no country representing more than 23% of revenue. This places it in a much stronger position than single-asset producers who risk revenue falling to zero overnight.

Conclusion

Pan American’s acquisition of MAG Silver marks a key addition to its portfolio. Pan American obtains a slice of a high-grade silver mine with some of the lowest costs in the world. This adds additional high-margin production in a mine with a long lifetime ahead. While the upfront cost is higher than previous acquisitions by Pan American and dilutes existing shareholders, in the long run, this looks highly accreditive with significant exploration upside potential.

With the latest results showing a strong performance, aided by the completion of new ventilation infrastructure at La Colorada, Pan American is in a strong position. Moreover, Pan American's continued share buybacks suggest management sees value at current levels and is actively working to offset any dilution.

With precious metal prices remaining near record levels, and one of the most diversified miners in the world, Pan American is well placed for the future. With my calculations continuing to suggest Pan American Silver remains undervalued at its current price, I continue to assign a buy rating.

data.title: Acadia Pharmaceuticals: Another Legal Win, ACP-101 Readout Preview
data.description:
Shares of Acadia Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:ACAD) rallied 26% after the company secured another court win that further solidified Nuplazid’s intellectual property position.

The stock is now back to the low 20s and new 52-week highs, and while I did remove Acadia from our Growth Stock Forum model portfolio in late 2024 as part of downsizing and reprioritization efforts, it remains part of my Coverage Universe and I continue to see the company as well positioned for long-term value creation based on the steadily growing revenue base and cash flows and an expanding pipeline with two important readouts in the next 12 months.

Enrollment in ACP-101’s phase 3 trial in Prader Willi syndrome patients is ahead of schedule and Acadia now expects to report topline results in Q4 2025 versus the previous guidance for the first half of 2026, and ACP-204 (the next-generation Nuplazid) is progressing towards the first phase 2/3 trial readout next year, and it is expanding to Lewy body dementia psychosis with a phase 2 trial expected to start in the third quarter.

Another legal win solidifies Nuplazid’s long IP runway

Acadia’s share price rose 26% on Friday after the company secured another legal win on Nuplazid. This is the so-called “Pimavanserin II” case.

The company announced that the U.S. District Court for the District of Delaware ruled in favor of Acadia on Nuplazid's ‘721 formulation patent. The court ruled in favor of Acadia on infringement and validity arguments in this case against Aurobindo Pharma and other ANDA filers. The ‘721 patent provides protection for the Nuplazid 34mg capsule formulation into 2038.

Acadia has previously settled with other generic filers that allowed the launch of the 34mg capsule in 2038 and of the 10mg tablet in September 2036, and this week’s court win ensures that this will remain the case (for the 34mg capsule) for generic filers that did not settle with the company.

What now remains on the legal side for Nuplazid is the appeal of MSN Pharmaceutical in the “Pimavanserin I” case related to patent ‘740 that Acadia won in late 2023. The appeal is fully briefed and the oral argument is scheduled for June 6, 2025.

Underwhelming but steady growth of commercial business

The bad news is that Daybue’s growth trajectory has not picked up in the last few quarters.

Acadia's increased efforts to improve Daybue’s traction and persistence are producing modest results. Net sales grew 11% Y/Y in the first quarter, and they declined sequentially due to the typical seasonal headwinds most pharmaceutical products face in the first quarter.

The good news is that Nuplazid is outperforming expectations and that total revenue growth expectations have finally found their footing. There were no downward revenue estimate revisions from analysts for the next four years in the last six months and expectations have slightly risen. The consensus for total revenues in the 2025-2028 period has improved by 1% to 4% compared to six months ago and by up to 1% in the last one and three months.

Rising expectations is one of the key ingredients for strong share price performance and while it is still largely lacking for Acadia, it is good to see that the downtrend was arrested and that it has slightly reversed.

I expect to see continued steady revenue growth going forward with potential acceleration in 2027 from potential new launches.

ACP-101’s phase 3 trial results are now expected in Q4 2025

The enrollment in ACP-101’s phase 3 trial is ahead of schedule and Acadia now expects to report topline results in the fourth quarter of this year versus the previous guidance for the first half of 2024.

I wrote about this trial in my February 2024 article to Growth Stock Forum subscribers and I will share a slightly abbreviated version today.

ACP-101 (formerly LV-101) is an intranasal version of carbetocin, an oxytocin analog designed to have improved oxytocin receptor selectivity and longer half-life compared with endogenous oxytocin. The candidate was gained from Levo Therapeutics in June 2022.

PWS is “characterized by profound hyperphagia, or an intense persistent sensation of hunger accompanied by food preoccupations and an extreme drive to consume food. Overt hyperphagia typically presents by 7 to 8 years of age and is associated with the lack of normal satiety response and a low basal metabolic rate.” Other psychiatric symptoms include increased anxiousness, obsessive-compulsive tendencies, and maladaptive behaviors. Oxytocin deficiency in these patients was believed to be associated with this constant sense of hunger in PWS patients.

ACP-101 is administered three times per day with meals. It was generally safe and well tolerated in phase 2 and phase 3 trials. Still, the phase 3 trial failed because the primary endpoints were based on the 9.6mg dose that did not statistically separate from placebo.

A positive efficacy signal was generated with the 3.2mg dose, and even though the effect of this dose was considered a secondary endpoint, Levo Therapeutics decided to submit the NDA. The Advisory Committee vote was negative (12 to 1 against approval) and the FDA ended up rejecting the application and asking for a new phase 3 trial in January 2022. Levo Therapeutics had no funds to do such a study and ultimately gave the rights to Acadia in June 2022 for $10 million upfront.

The efficacy signal of the 3.2mg dose looked good in the phase 3 trial, but the inverse dose-response is not exactly encouraging, nor is the failure of the 9.6mg dose to separate from placebo. Levo argued that the drug candidate has a U-shaped dose-response, and the FDA acknowledged the possibility but said these types of responses are rare.

The 9.6mg dose showed a trend on the HQ-CT endpoint in the phase 3 trial but it was far from reaching statistical significance and the effect of the 3.2mg seemed robust with a p-value of 0.016. The 3.2mg dose also achieved statistical significance on several secondary endpoints and it showed improvements in others compared to placebo. The table below shows how the 9.6mg dose also showed improvements on all endpoints, but without reaching statistical significance on any of the endpoints.

Responder analyses also favored the 3.2mg dose over the 9.6mg dose and placebo and the additional evidence of the 3.2mg dose working well was the placebo crossover period where patients crossing to the 3.2mg dose experienced significant improvements on the primary endpoint while the patients receiving the 9.6mg dose had no change.

There was also continued separation of the 3.2mg and 9.6mg doses in favor of the 3.2mg dose in the open-label extension part of the phase 3 trial.

The new phase 3 trial is slightly different and this adds to the risk of this phase 3 trial.

The length of treatment was extended from 8 weeks to 12 weeks and only the 3.2mg dose will be tested against placebo instead of two separate treatment groups. There is only one primary endpoint – the Hyperphagia Questionnaire for Clinical Trials, or HQ-CT, instead of two in the previous trial with CY-BOCS being relegated to a secondary endpoint.

Overall, I see a decent amount of evidence that supports further investigation of ACP-101 in PWS patients, but see the probability of success as below average for a phase 3 trial because of the issues from previous trials that I outlined here.

However, I do not see success of this trial as priced in at current levels and believe the risk-reward is good for patient long-term investors because I expect Acadia to do well in the long run even if ACP-101 fails and is discontinued.

In the meantime, we should see early evidence of commercial viability of PWS drugs from the launch of Soleno Therapeutics’ (SLNO) Vykat XR, which was approved by the FDA in March.

Risks

With most of the IP risks on Nuplazid in the rearview mirror, the key risks are the performance of the commercial business worsening and clinical trial failures, mainly of ACP-101 in PWS in Q4 2025 and of ACP-204 in Alzheimer's disease psychosis in mid-2026.

The standard biopharma macro risks also apply to Acadia, primarily potential pricing pressure for its drugs. However, I believe the risks to Acadia are lower than the average biopharma company because Nuplazid is not sold outside of the U.S. and is not subject to Most Favored Nation pricing risk that has resurfaced again, and with Acadia being well aware of such risks, it is unlikely to price Daybue far from the U.S. price in ex-U.S. territories.

Conclusion

Acadia's fundamentals are in better shape after another court win that all but secures Nuplazid's exclusivity until 2036 and after the stabilization of the commercial business following Daybue's worsening trajectory during late 2023 and the first half of 2025.

The key clinical catalyst is now expected in Q4 2025 with ACP-101 phase 3 data in PWS patients, and while I view it as not critical for the company's long-term success and the trial itself having below average probability of success based on the issues I outlined, I see the risk-reward as favorable for long-term investors that are willing to hold the stock beyond this event.

data.title: Fraport: Focus On Cash Flow Amidst High Debt Load
data.description:
Operating an airport is a lucrative business with high margins. So, they are quite appealing for investment. However, at the same time we do note that airports continue to invest in new projects and also spend significant amounts of money on maintenance. This provides a risk as new projects may face delays or higher input costs. Furthermore, airport operations are highly dependent on demand for air travel.

Fraport (OTCPK:FPRUF, OTCPK:FPRUY) is a company that is coming out of its CapEx heavy phase and has called off some smaller projects in an effort to bolster its cash position amidst rising labor costs. I covered the company in February and the stock has done rather well with a 15% return for the OTC ticker. The listing in Frankfurt (FWB:FRA) lost around 1%. So, there is a discrepancy between the OTC stock price development and the development of the underlying instrument. Around 5 points out of the 15% return is driven by exchange rate changes with the remainder being driven by lower volumes which at times causes the stock price not being reflective of the underlying instrument. That is also a reason why I believe that for serious investment considerations one should elect to invest in the most liquid instrument, which in the case of Fraport is the listing in Germany.

In this report, I discuss the most recent earnings, assess the guidance and the risks and opportunities that the company may face.

Fraport Shows Strong Performance Despite Slow Recovery At Frankfurt

Reported revenues were up 6% excluding construction revenues to €811 million. EBITDA declined 17% to €178 million. Excluding one offs, EBITDA would have been stable year-on-year. It is not the kind of performance we are looking for, but we do note that Frankfurt posted a decline in passenger traffic driven by weaker demand for destinations in the Middle East and West Europe. Generally, all European airports except for the ones in Greece posted declines in passenger traffic. While the Brazilian airports saw traffic increase except for Porto Alegre where traffic is still ramping up after the temporary flight suspension following the floods in 2024.

The increase in revenues was in part driven by high airport charges, fees and ground services. Frankfurt accounted for nearly 60% of the increase in adjusted revenues through higher charges and unit revenues. The remainder came most notably from Lima accounting for 20% of the increase while Fraport Greece and Fraport USA accounted for around 15% of the increase in revenues.

Operating expenses adjusted for construction costs increased 8.6% to €657.1 million due to higher concession charges driven by traffic volumes and higher labor costs. This was partially driven by higher headcounts, but we also note that the company reached a wage agreement effective from the first of April raising wages by 3% with a minimum increase of €110 per month and next year there will be another step up of 2.8% and an increase in the annual bonus. So, Fraport is facing labor costs pressures. I do expect the company to be able to pass through some of these pressures but that does not take away from the fact that in a more uncertain environment costs are rising.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Fraport?

For the full year, Frankfurt expects 64 million passengers which is an increase of nearly 4%. Compared to the pre-pandemic traffic volumes, the company is only 90% recovered. The slow recovery is driven by numerous factors. One of the factors is the soft demand from flights to China and in Q1 we saw soft demand for destinations in the Middle East and West Europe. Furthermore, operators including Lufthansa is facing delivery delays on narrow body and wide body airplanes which is constraining growth at the Frankfurt hub. Wizz Air is facing delivery delays as well which primarily affects the airports in Bulgaria (Burgas and Varna) while these Sunny Beach destinations also do not seem to be preferred leisure destinations.

We also have to monitor the geopolitical and trade setting, which next to adverse weather, can affect operations and demand. Costs at Frankfurt continue to rise due to labor costs and requirements to blend in Sustainable Aviation Fuel, which does increase the costs of operations for airlines in Frankfurt.

EBITDA is still expected to grow modestly this year supported by the opening of the new terminal at Lima in May and the new terminal opened in Antalya. Terminal 3 in Frankfurt will be opened next year allowing for a revamp of Terminal 2, which will have its operations moved to Terminal 3. Given that Frankfurt is not yet operating at full capacity, this temporary closure of Terminal 2 should pose no issues from capacity perspective.

As growth is somewhat challenged this year on group level, the company has elected to adapt its CapEx plan accordingly. The company is focusing on quick win projects with lower capital need while it extended the planning phase for the Terminal 2 revamp, effectively reducing near-term CapEx.

So, the company has some growth ahead through the recovery of Frankfurt and the new terminal at Lima but we also see some challenges due to labor increases and macroeconomic pressures.

Fraport Stock Has Upside Amidst Challenges

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The median EV/EBITDA for Fraport would suggest a negative stock price valuation due to the high debt load that the company is facing. However, the debt has been compounded through the CapEx heavy phase which is now largely behind us. Particularly from FY26 onward we should be seeing the company being able to significantly reduce its gross debt and leverage should already come down this year.

This year, the company will be refinancing the majority of its Frankfurt related debt maturities this year. In combination with the rough operating environment where Frankfurt is not quite living up to recovery expectations while costs are rising, I am assigning a hold rating with a retrospective price target of $71.05, which is a significant reduction from the prior target of $85.15. This is driven by lower peer group median that I am applying to transportation infrastructure names.

The book value per share is $57.10. Typically the company trades at 1.4x its book value and the peer group trades at 1.26x book value. That would imply a stock price target of $71.94 to $80. With that in mind, I am maintaining my buy rating but I am lowering my price target to $71.05.

Conclusion: Fraport Will Face A Challenging Year

I believe that Fraport will face a challenging year as its Frankfurt operations are not quite recovering amidst geopolitical challenges as well as shortages of airplanes and higher costs. However, from valuation point-of-view I do believe that the typical price-to-book valuation provides upside and enough reason to maintain my buy rating. I also believe that part of this year’s debt maturities will be refinanced for the sole purpose to build in some liquidity to pay a dividend after 2025.

In some way, for Fraport despite the challenges it is harvesting season. CapEx is moderating and the focus is now on improving the free cash flow.
